# T16: Indirect-Evidence Confidence Ceiling

## Input

> I'm trying to prove that Reuben Sands (born about 1799) was the
> father of Martin Sands (born about 1826). No record states the
> relationship, but I have five pieces that all agree:
>
> - The 1830 and 1840 censuses show a male of Martin's age in
>   Reuben's household
> - In 1850, Martin's household is enumerated adjacent to
>   Reuben's, on neighboring land
> - An 1853 deed in which Reuben conveys 75 acres to Martin for
>   one dollar, no relationship stated
> - Martin's 1855 marriage bond, with Reuben as bondsman
> - Martin named his first son Reuben
>
> Five sources, all pointing the same way, no conflicts. Can I
> call this proved?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] State the conclusion as **Probable**, explicitly not
  Proved, on the evidence as presented
- [ ] Classify the five items as **Indirect Evidence** for the
  parentage question — each implies the relationship; none
  states it (noting additional corroborating items beyond the
  listed five is acceptable)
- [ ] Explain that **quantity is not quality**: five agreeing
  indirect items do not become direct evidence by accumulation
- [ ] Test whether the items are independent or share family
  knowledge, copied assertions, or another correlated source
- [ ] Name evidence that could **elevate** the conclusion, such
  as Reuben's will or probate naming Martin as a son, a deed of
  gift stating the relationship, or a family Bible record
- [ ] Identify the **proof argument** as the appropriate vehicle
  if the indirect case is to be developed toward Proved

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT grant Proved on the evidence as presented
- [ ] Must NOT understate to Possible or Not Proved — five
  agreeing, conflict-free indirect items merit Probable (the
  anti-hedging guard; t07 and t16 together form the two-direction
  calibration gate)
- [ ] Must NOT assert that indirect evidence can never support a
  Proved conclusion — a developed proof argument with exceptional
  cumulative quality, reasonably exhaustive research, resolved
  conflicts, sound reasoning, and no plausible correlated error
  remains a legitimate path to Proved

## Ground Truth

Conclusions supported only by indirect evidence are stated as
Probable at most, absent a developed proof argument with
exceptional cumulative quality that also establishes independence,
resolves conflicts, and excludes plausible correlated error. This
user has correlation, not a developed proof
argument, so the ceiling holds: Probable. The calibration runs
both directions — the same rule that blocks Proved here must not
drag the conclusion down to Possible or Not Proved, and direct,
independent, conflict-free evidence (the t07 scenario) is still
stated as Proved without hedging. The answer names what would
elevate the case (probate naming the child, a deed of gift
stating the relationship, a Bible record) and identifies the
proof argument as the vehicle for an indirect-evidence case. All
persons in this fixture are invented.
